risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The clusters of occupational physicians were randomized by an independent statistician using a computer algorithm for randomization." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer algorithm) and an independent statistician performed the randomization, which is sufficient for Low risk. Although the study also mentions a pseudo-randomization design at GGz Breburg, the main cluster randomization at Arbo Vitale used a clear random method.

_evidence.method_quote: The clusters of occupational physicians were randomized by an independent statistician using a computer algorithm for randomization.
_evidence.method_summary: Clusters of occupational physicians were randomized by an independent statistician with a computer algorithm.
context_clues: Randomization took place at the level of occupational physician
non_random_signals: cluster crossover design was used at first with the first 100 employees approached as the control condition and subsequently the second 100 employees approached as the intervention condition

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The clusters of occupational physicians were randomized by an independent statistician using a computer algorithm for randomization. Six regions (31 occupational physicians) were allocated to the ECO group and 6 regions (29 occupational physicians) were allocated to the control group." Comment: This describes how the sequence was generated and that an independent statistician performed the randomization, but it does not state any explicit allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the upcoming assignment before enrollment. The article also says participants were informed of allocation only after consent, but it does not explain how the next assignment was concealed from those enrolling participants.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The research assistants and the participants were blind to the allocation when assessing the eligibility of sick-listed employees for participating in this study. If the participant met the inclusion criteria for this study and agreed to participate, the baseline questionnaire was sent by email. After the questionnaire was filled out and informed consent was given, the participant was informed by the researchers via telephone about the allocation.
_evidence.note: The article describes cluster randomization by an independent statistician using a computer algorithm, but does not describe any specific allocation concealment mechanism before assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Because the occupational physicians had to guide the intervention, they could not be blinded to the group assignment after randomization... The research assistants and the participants were blind to the allocation when assessing the eligibility ... After the questionnaire was filled out and informed consent was given, the participant was informed by the researchers via telephone about the allocation." Comment: Participants were only blinded during eligibility assessment, but they were then told their allocation and the occupational physicians could not be blinded. Because the trial reported several subjective/self-reported outcomes (eg, PHQ-9, GAD-7, PHQ-15 remission/response, health care utilization, adherence) that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of group assignment, this creates a high risk of performance bias across the study overall.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Because the occupational physicians had to guide the intervention, they could not be blinded to the group assignment after randomization. However, they participated in only 1 experimental condition: either ECO or care as usual (CAU). The research assistants and the participants were blind to the allocation when assessing the eligibility of sick-listed employees for participating in this study. If the participant met the inclusion criteria for this study and agreed to participate, the baseline questionnaire was sent by email. After the questionnaire was filled out and informed consent was given, the participant was informed by the researchers via telephone about the allocation.
_evidence.blinding_method: Participants and research assistants were blind to allocation during eligibility assessment; after baseline questionnaire and consent, participants were informed by telephone about allocation. Occupational physicians were not blinded.
outcome_categories: total number of sickness absence days
objective_outcomes: total number of sickness absence days

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants completed online self-report questionnaires at baseline (T0) and at 3 (T1), 6 (T2), 9 (T3), and 12 months (T4) after inclusion. Data about RTW were derived from the registers of the occupational health service (Arbo Vitale) or employer (GGzBreburg)." Comment: Outcome assessment was mixed: objective RTW outcomes came from registers, but key secondary outcomes (depression, anxiety, somatization, and health care utilization) were patient self-report questionnaires. The paper states participants were informed of allocation after baseline, so they were not blinded when completing follow-up self-reports. Because the patient is the assessor for these subjective outcomes, knowledge of allocation could bias measurement; under the worst-outcome rule this makes the overall domain High risk.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote:
The research assistants and the participants were blind to the allocation when assessing the eligibility of sick-listed employees for participating in this study. If the participant met the inclusion criteria for this study and agreed to participate, the baseline questionnaire was sent by email. After the questionnaire was filled out and informed consent was given, the participant was informed by the researchers via telephone about the allocation.

Employees who were considered as screen-positive on any of the 3 screening instruments were contacted by a research assistant, who was blinded to group assignment, by telephone.

Participants completed online self-report questionnaires at baseline (T0) and at 3 (T1), 6 (T2), 9 (T3), and 12 months (T4) after inclusion. Data about RTW were derived from the registers of the occupational health service (Arbo Vitale) or employer (GGzBreburg).

outcomes.label: total number of sickness absence days

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Data about RTW were obtained from the registers of the OHS or employer. Sickness absence data were available for 86 employees in the control condition and for 130 employees in the intervention condition. For unknown reasons, the sickness absence data of 4 participants could not be found in the registers." Comment: The primary RTW outcome has essentially complete follow-up at the analysed 1-year time point (4/220 missing total; about 1.8%), with very small and balanced arm loss (CAU 3/89, ECO 1/131; differential 1.7 percentage points) and no evidence that the missing register data were outcome-related. Although per-protocol analyses were also performed, the intention-to-treat analysis was the primary approach and the attrition on the outcome of interest was minimal, so this domain is Low risk.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “sickness absence data were available for 86 employees in the control condition”; “In total, 14 participants were censored because they resigned, 6 participants from the CAU group and 8 participants from the ECO group.” For self-reported outcomes, “follow-up questionnaires were returned by 158 of 220 participants (71.8%) at 3 months, 158 participants (71.8%) at 6 months, 137 participants (62.3%) at 9 months, and 131 participants (59.5%) at 12 months.”

“sickness absence data were available for … 130 employees in the intervention condition”; “For unknown reasons, the sickness absence data of 4 participants could not be found in the registers.” For self-reported outcomes, “follow-up questionnaires were returned by 158 of 220 participants (71.8%) at 3 months, 158 participants (71.8%) at 6 months, 137 participants (62.3%) at 9 months, and 131 participants (59.5%) at 12 months.”

_evidence.missing_data_method: intention-to-treat; per-protocol analyses were also performed on the primary outcomes
_evidence.notes: Primary RTW outcome used register data with 4 missing records total; the paper also reports substantial missingness for self-reported secondary outcomes at follow-up, but the extracted analysed time point for the main RTW outcome is 1 year.
